summary: Gray is my mother's mother's maiden name, so the family on that side were all Grays. When I was born, my parents struggled with naming me, and I was almost named Sam after my great uncle Sam Gray. The Grays are part of my heritage, but I never met my great uncle Sam East, who was a railroad man.

My family came over from France, arriving at Ellis Island before settling in the United States. This French heritage is part of my family history, although I never met my grandfather Sam East who was part of this lineage. The journey from France to Ellis Island marks the beginning of my family's American story.

Ellis Island was the entry point for my ancestors who came from France. It was through Ellis Island that my family entered the United States before moving inland. This place represents the start of their American journey and the roots of my family here.

My grandfather, Sam East, worked primarily for the Missouri Pacific Railroad. He was a railroad man before later working for the Midland railroad based in Muskogee. His career with Missouri Pacific was an important part of his life, though I never met him personally.

After working for the Missouri Pacific Railroad, my grandfather Sam East worked for the Midland railroad, which was based in Muskogee. This railroad was part of his career in the rail industry, connecting him to the Muskogee area where I was born and raised. The Midland railroad was a key part of his professional life.

I was born in Muskogee, Oklahoma, at Baptist Hospital in 1954 and spent my early years there. My mother eventually worked as a teacher in Muskogee, and my father lived there with his family after returning from the war. Muskogee is where much of my childhood and family life took place.

After growing up in Muskogee, I went off to Oklahoma State University for my higher education. This was a significant step in my life, moving from my hometown to pursue further studies. OSU marked the beginning of my adult academic journey.

My mother, Marjorie Sugg, grew up in Ada, Oklahoma, where she attended school before becoming a teacher. Ada was her hometown and the place where her family roots were strong. It was also where she developed the extroverted and personable traits that defined her teaching career.

My grandmother on my mother's side came from Tennessee. She was part of the hardworking family background that influenced my mother and me. Tennessee is part of my maternal heritage and family history.

My father grew up in Arkansas, specifically in a small town called Norman, which is south of Mount Ida. His family were farmers before his father began working for the railroad, which led them to move around Arkansas and Oklahoma. Arkansas is where my father's roots and early life were established.

Norman, Arkansas, is the small town where my father grew up. It's located south of Mount Ida and was part of his family's farming background before they transitioned to railroad work. Norman represents my father's early life and rural upbringing.

Mount Ida, Arkansas, is a nearby town to Norman, where my father grew up. People from Tulsa often drove through Mount Ida on their way to Hot Springs, so it was a known landmark in the region. It situates my father's childhood area in Arkansas.

After my grandfather started working for the railroad, my father's family moved around, living in places including Pawhuska, Oklahoma. This was one of the towns where my father spent part of his youth before settling in Muskogee. Pawhuska is part of the family's movement tied to railroad work.

My father's maintenance business had a counterpart in Oklahoma City, which was part of a three-location agreement to avoid competition. The Oklahoma City business was owned by the family of a former mayor of Newark, and it was one of the regional hubs alongside Muskogee and Arkansas. Oklahoma City was part of the broader business network my father was involved with.

As time went on, businesses like Target Corporation began selling directly to schools and organizations, which affected my father's maintenance business. When I was a kid, stores like Target didn't exist, so my father's business filled that niche. Target represents the changing retail landscape that eventually impacted our family business.

Walmart, along with Target, became a major retailer that schools and businesses could buy from directly, which contributed to the decline of my father's maintenance business. When I was growing up, Walmart was not around yet, but it later became a dominant force in retail. Walmart symbolizes the evolution of commerce that changed how products were distributed.

On a trip to visit my father's grandmother in Norman, Arkansas, we stopped in Poteau, Oklahoma, at the Black Angus restaurant. It was there that I met Senator Bob Kerr, who owned the restaurant at the time. Poteau was a memorable stop on that family journey.

The Black Angus restaurant in Poteau, Oklahoma, was owned by Senator Bob Kerr when we stopped there for lunch on a family trip. It was the only time I met Senator Kerr, who came over to greet my mother. The restaurant was a notable place connected to local politics and community.

Bob Kerr was a U.S. senator who also owned the Black Angus restaurant in Poteau, Oklahoma. I met him once when he came over to say hello to my mother during a family lunch there. He grew up in Ada, Oklahoma, like my mother, and was known for helping people during tough times.

Harbord Presnell Sugg, or 'HP' as most called him, was my maternal grandfather and a state representative during World War II. He served three terms and was known for helping people with insurance and Indian trust matters. His political career and community service influenced my early interest in government and politics.

McAlester, Oklahoma, was part of the political district that included my grandfather's legislative area. Powerful people from McAlester came to influence the redistricting that ended my grandfather's political career. McAlester played a role in the political landscape my family was involved in.

Pittsburgh County, Oklahoma, was part of the legislative district my grandfather represented. During redistricting, it was combined with Pontotoc County, affecting my grandfather's decision not to run again. Pittsburgh County was central to my family's political history.

Pontotoc County, Oklahoma, was merged with Pittsburgh County during a mid-year redistricting that influenced my grandfather's political career. This change brought new political players into the district, including Gene Stipe. Pontotoc County was part of the shifting political boundaries affecting my family.

Gene Stipe was a young man from McAlester who came into my grandfather's district during redistricting and later became a state senator. I interviewed Senator Stipe periodically over many years, and while we were not friends, we maintained a cordial relationship. He was part of the political environment that shaped my family's history.

I attended Irving Elementary School in Muskogee, Oklahoma, for my early education. It was the start of my schooling before moving on to junior high and high school in the same city. Irving Elementary was where my academic journey began.

After elementary school, I went to Alice Robertson Junior High School in Muskogee. This was part of my formative years before high school, continuing my education locally. Alice Robertson Junior High was a key step in my schooling.

I graduated from Muskogee High School during its second year of full integration. Being part of the integration process was significant and sometimes tense, but it went smoothly under the leadership of Superintendent John Tom Staten. I was very active in student government and even started an underground newspaper during my junior year.

John Tom Staten was the superintendent of Muskogee schools during the integration of Muskogee High School, which I attended. He handled the process marvelously, setting up structures to prevent disturbances. Later, he became a legislator, and his leadership had a lasting impact on the community.
